answers.value: Patient is a 72-year-old male with a 9-year history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and longstanding hypertension, now with ESRD on thrice-weekly in-center hemodialysis since 2021. He has had progressive anemia of CKD despite iron repletion, with fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and decreased ability to attend full dialysis sessions without resting.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: He was previously managed on epoetin alfa (EPOGEN) 8,000 units IV three times weekly for approximately 10 months. Despite dose escalation and confirmed adequate iron stores (ferritin persistently 350–550 ng/mL, TSAT 28–35%), his hemoglobin remained labile (ranging 8.1–9.3 g/dL) with frequent dips below 9 g/dL, requiring two PRBC transfusions over the past year (2024-02 and 2024-07) when Hgb fell to 7.8 and 7.6 g/dL respectively.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: He also experienced significant blood pressure surges during and immediately after epoetin administration (intradialytic systolic BP spikes to 190–200 mmHg from baseline 140–150 mmHg) and new-onset headaches on dialysis days. Given his history of non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy and prior lacunar stroke in 2020, minimizing further transfusions and avoiding wide hemoglobin swings is clinically important. Goal is to maintain a more stable Hgb 10–11 g/dL with fewer hypertensive episodes and reduced transfusion risk.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value:
Requesting an exception to step therapy and quantity limits to allow continued use of darbepoetin alfa (ARANESP) at the current dose and interval. The patient has already undergone an adequate and prolonged trial of epoetin alfa with suboptimal response and clinically significant side effects, including poorly controlled intradialytic hypertension, recurrent headaches, and two documented blood transfusions in the last 12 months due to hemoglobin dropping below 8 g/dL despite adherence.

Given his age (72), history of prior ischemic stroke, and diabetic retinopathy, wide fluctuations in hemoglobin and frequent transfusions pose increased risk of cardiovascular and cerebrovascular events, as well as alloimmunization that could complicate potential future transplant evaluation. Since transitioning to ARANESP 60 mcg IV once weekly (initiated 2024-08-15), his hemoglobin has stabilized between 10.2–10.8 g/dL over three consecutive monthly checks, with markedly fewer intradialytic BP spikes and improved energy level as reported by both patient and dialysis nursing staff.

Forcing a return to epoetin or a lower-than-clinically-indicated ARANESP dose would likely destabilize his anemia control, increase the risk of further transfusions, and worsen quality of life. For these reasons, we are requesting that the plan waive any remaining step requirements and allow coverage for ARANESP at the current dose and frequency as medically necessary for ongoing anemia management in ESRD on hemodialysis.

answers.value: Darbepoetin alfa (ARANESP) has demonstrated superior clinical effectiveness and tolerability for this specific patient compared to epoetin alfa. On epoetin, his hemoglobin remained unstable with frequent drops below target, necessitating transfusions and contributing to fatigue and reduced participation in activities of daily living.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Since the switch to ARANESP 60 mcg IV weekly, his hemoglobin has stabilized in the desired 10–11 g/dL range with fewer dose adjustments, reduced intradialytic blood pressure spikes, and improved symptom control (less shortness of breath when walking from the parking lot to the dialysis unit, improved ability to complete full 4-hour dialysis sessions without interruption, and better appetite). The longer half-life of darbepoetin has allowed more consistent erythropoiesis with fewer peaks and troughs, which is particularly important in a patient with prior stroke and cardiovascular risk factors.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.value: Nursing documentation and patient self-report both indicate improved energy on non-dialysis days, fewer missed dialysis treatments, and more stable post-dialysis blood pressures since conversion to ARANESP. No adverse reactions to ARANESP have been observed. Given his prior inadequate response and side effects on epoetin, along with the documented clinical stability achieved on ARANESP, this medication represents the most appropriate and effective erythropoiesis-stimulating agent for his ongoing anemia management in ESRD.

answers.value: Altering the current ARANESP regimen—either by discontinuing ARANESP and reverting to epoetin or by significantly lowering the ARANESP dose—would likely destabilize his anemia control and recreate the pattern of hemoglobin variability previously seen. This could lead to recurrent symptomatic anemia, increased transfusion requirements, and associated risks (volume overload, iron overload, alloimmunization, transfusion reactions).

answers.value: The patient has a prior history of intradialytic hypertension and headaches temporally associated with higher-dose epoetin administration, which improved after transitioning to ARANESP. Reintroducing epoetin or under-treating his anemia would risk recurrence of these adverse events and could further compromise his cardiovascular status in the setting of long-standing diabetes, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy on echocardiogram, and prior lacunar stroke.

answers.value: Furthermore, any significant hemoglobin decline could worsen his already limited exercise tolerance, increase his risk of falls, and reduce adherence to dialysis due to fatigue and malaise, ultimately increasing hospitalizations. Maintaining his current stable ARANESP regimen is therefore clinically important not only for symptom control but also for preventing serious downstream complications and preserving his functional independence.
